nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; found over reefs and rocky bottoms at depths of roughly 2–70 m. Adults commonly form aggregations near ledges and caves during the day and disperse to forage over adjacent sand and seagrass at night.

diet.notes: In the wild, has been documented feeding at night on mollusks, echinoderms, annelids, and small crustaceans. In captivity, generally accepts a variety of meaty marine foods including chopped shrimp, squid, scallops, mussel, and marine fish flesh, along with prepared frozen carnivore foods and sinking pellets. Has been reported to also accept algae-based prepared foods, so offering some vegetable matter alongside meaty fare is commonly recommended. Juveniles act as facultative cleaners, picking parasites from larger fish.

avoidWith: very small or timid fish (juvenile bullying)

summary: The Porkfish (Anisotremus virginicus) is a peaceful Caribbean grunt reaching 25-40 cm, recognized by two black bars on a yellow-and-blue striped body. Generally considered fish-only-with-live-rock; not reef-safe due to appetite for ornamental invertebrates.
careNotes:
The Porkfish is a hardy Western Atlantic grunt that tends to do well in fish-only or fish-only-with-live-rock systems. It is generally regarded as peaceful toward unrelated tankmates but has been known to show some aggression toward conspecifics in cramped quarters, so adequate space and refuge structure are commonly advised. Live rock arranged into caves, ledges, and overhangs provides daytime shelter, while ample open swimming room is generally recommended for adults, which can reach roughly 30-40 cm.

This species is not considered reef-safe: it has been reported to readily consume ornamental shrimp, small crabs, snails, and starfish, and may sample sessile invertebrates. Pairing with peaceful larger fish (tangs, angelfishes, peaceful triggerfish, groupers) is commonly recommended over delicate or very small tankmates, which juveniles can also be intimidated by.

Porkfish are members of the grunt family (Haemulidae) and produce audible grunting sounds by grinding pharyngeal teeth, with the swim bladder acting as a resonator. Juveniles are visually distinct from adults — they display a dark lateral stripe and a caudal-base spot rather than the adult's black bars and yellow-blue stripes — and have been documented behaving as facultative cleaners, picking parasites from larger reef fish.

Source notes: LiveAquaria lists the maximum size as approximately 30 cm (12 in) and recommends a 180-gallon (~680 L) tank with care level "Easy"; BlueZoo Aquatics reports up to 40 cm (16 in) with 175+ gallons recommended for adults and an "Intermediate" care rating; FishBase records a maximum total length of 40.6 cm (common 25 cm) and a preferred temperature range of 24.9-28.2 °C. The values stored here represent averages across these sources; tank size has been biased upward to 700 L given the species' adult size, active swimming, and the prose cues about ample swimming space. Care level was set to intermediate as the average of the two retailer ratings.

Diet classification varies: LiveAquaria describes the species as accepting both meaty and vegetable foods while BlueZoo and FishBase document it as a nocturnal carnivore on small invertebrates. Recorded here as carnivore consistent with the biological data, with the note that algae-based prepared foods may also be accepted.
breedingNotes: Breeding in home aquaria has not been widely documented. FishBase notes distinct pairing during breeding in the wild, but the species' adult size and shoaling habit make captive spawning generally impractical for typical home setups.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Specific product page (https://www.liveaquaria.com/product/374/) provided care level (Easy), max size 12 in (~30 cm), 180 gal (~680 L) minimum tank, temperament Peaceful, reef-safe No, temperature 72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked sizing, temperament, diet, tank size. Reported max size 16 in (~40.6 cm), 50 gal juvenile / 175+ gal adult tank, peaceful with conspecific aggression in cramped conditions, reef-safe No, care level Intermediate, carnivorous (mollusks/crustaceans/small invertebrates). Confirmed juvenile cleaner behavior and intra-species aggression in tight quarters.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed family Haemulidae, native Western Atlantic (Florida-Brazil, Caribbean, Gulf of Mexico, Bermuda introduced), max length 40.6 cm TL / common 25 cm, depth 2-70 m, preferred temperature 24.9-28.2 °C, nocturnal carnivore on mollusks/echinoderms/annelids/crustaceans, distinct pairing during breeding. Original Reef2Reef wiki secondary returned 403; FishBase substituted as a third confirming source.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been widely documented; adult size and shoaling habits make home-aquarium spawning generally impractical.
